steps.content: A boolean is a data type with only two possible values: True or False. Named after the mathematician George Boole, booleans are Python's way of representing truth values. You actually saw booleans briefly in the variables lesson when we discussed data types — now it's time to really understand them.

is_sunny = True
is_raining = False

print(is_sunny)
print(is_raining)
print(type(is_sunny))
Booleans are created whenever you compare two values using comparison operators. These operators check a relationship and return True or False. Here are the most important ones:

a = 10
b = 5

print(a == b)
print(a != b)
print(a > b)
print(a < b)
print(a >= 10)
print(b <= 3)
You can also compare strings. Python compares them alphabetically using the Unicode order of each character. This builds on what you learned in the strings lesson.
Don't confuse = (assignment) with == (comparison). A single = sets a value, while == checks if two values are equal. This is one of the most common beginner mistakes!

Python's built-in bool() function converts any value into a boolean. This reveals an important concept: every value in Python is either **truthy** or **falsy**. The following values are considered False:

items: None — the absence of a value (from the variables lesson)
[] — an empty list (you haven't met lists yet, but quick heads-up: they're falsy too)

steps.content:
print(bool(1))
print(bool(0))
print(bool('hello'))
print(bool(''))
print(bool(None))
print(bool(-5))

Everything else is truthy! Non-empty strings, non-zero numbers, even the string 'False' (because it's a non-empty string) all convert to True. This will be very useful when we learn about conditionals next.
